summary: The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a special meeting for the 2024 tax budget public hearing. Administrator Bob Rose presented a preliminary 30,000-foot overview of the 2024 tax budget across all departments including general fund, zoning, police, service, and fire. Key discussions included transferring HR costs to restricted funds, using ARPA funds to supplement the general fund, proposed police union raises of 18% over three years with elimination of one position, skipping paving in 2024 to save for an OPWC grant match in 2025, and the upcoming need for the fire department to replace its five-year levy.
decisions: Adopted the agenda for the special meeting
Opened the public hearing on the 2024 tax budget
Closed the public hearing on the 2024 tax budget
Adjourned the special meeting at 6:37 PM
action_items: Administrator Rose and Fiscal Officer Laura to research whether HR cost transfers from restricted funds (fire, police, service) to general fund can be done directly or must be handled through payroll allocation
Administrator Rose to work with Trustee Allen on the proper procedure for transferring restricted fund monies
Department heads to prepare detailed budget recommendations for October/November presentation
Chad (Service Director) to explore lease options and used vehicles for dump truck acquisition
Administrator Rose to apply for body camera grant from the state if available
Fire Chief Myers to approach trustees about replacing the five-year fire and EMS levy
Staff to conduct cleanup day debriefing and prepare recommended plan for next year
Chad and Bob to continue planning for OPWC grant application for 2025 paving
key_amounts: $89,000 expected local government fund revenue for 2024
$30,000 HR cost transfer split among fire ($19,800), police ($8,000), and service ($2,000)
$45,000 budgeted for general fund contracted services (down from $78,000 in 2023)
$66,000 transfer from general fund to zoning (down from $84,000 in 2023)
$150,000 conservatively estimated zoning revenue for 2023
$20,000 budgeted for basement renovations from ARPA funds
$9,500 estimated for cleanup day from ARPA funds
$219,000 estimated remaining ARPA funds after proposed spending
$14,000-$15,000 in opioid settlement funds
$790,000 anticipated safety services levy revenue split 50/50 between police and fire
$451,000 police share of safety services levy
$85,000 estimated for body camera contract renewal (5-year)
18% raises over three years for police officers per proposed union contract
$800,000 spent on paving in current year (historic high for the township)
$75,000 set aside for 2025 paving
$109/ton salt price (up from $82/ton)
$115,000 school resource officer contract
$250,000 anticipated fire department ending unencumbered balance
$3.1 million estimated overall township ending balance for 2024
$8.7 million total estimated revenue, $8.8 million total estimated expenses
$35,000 fire and EMS building repair line item
$922,000 starting unencumbered for administration/zoning, declining to $879,000
notable_topics: 2024 tax budget public hearing and preliminary budget presentation
HR cost allocation across fire, police, and service departments
ARPA fund usage strategy for IT, cleanup day, and building improvements
Zoning department revenue increase from new subdivisions and fee schedule adjustments
Zoning consultant model replacing director position to save costs
Police union contract with 18% raises over three years and elimination of one position
Officer retention challenges due to pay gap with neighboring jurisdictions
Paving strategy - skipping 2024 to save for 2025 OPWC grant match
Salt cost increases from $82 to $109 per ton
Fire and EMS levy replacement needed as current five-year levy nears end
Cemetery fee adjustments and mausoleum success
General fund sustainability concerns - revenue vs expenses
notes: Trustee Hausermann/Halderman name appears inconsistently in the transcript - called both 'Hausermann' and 'Halderman' at different points. Fire Chief Myers was absent from the meeting. The ARPA funds must be spent by 2026. The opioid settlement will continue for approximately 25 years. Trustee Allen raised concerns about ensuring revenue exceeds expenses in the final budget and questioned the procedural legality of transferring from restricted funds to the general fund based on prior experience. The police department has lost several officers to higher-paying departments with $5-7/hour pay differences. This budget presentation is preliminary - the true budget will be presented in October/November.
title: BOT Special Meeting 6-27-23
